narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 65% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows you ranked in the lowest decile for grades in your first year of school and second-lowest in your third year, well below the typical student's mid-range performance. Your LSAT score of 26 is also notably lower than the average of around 37, which stands out as a key concern. On brighter notes, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 exceeds the typical 3.21, your family falls into a middle-income bracket similar to most applicants, you're an Asian female planning full-time studies—aligning with common demographics—and full-time status matches over 90% of students. When the model weighed these details against historical data, your third-year school decile ranking had the strongest influence, nudging the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind it, your LSAT score played a major role in pushing the odds in that direction. Your first-year decile came next in influence, also contributing to the failure risk. Family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status followed in descending order of impact, each slightly reinforcing the model's concern about passing the bar. Compared to typical students—who often have stronger mid-decile rankings and higher LSATs—your early and later school performance signals higher risk, despite your solid college grades. This ranking of influences highlights where the model saw the biggest gaps relative to those who pass. To boost your future bar exam chances, focus on addressing those low school deciles by building stronger study habits and time management skills, perhaps through targeted prep courses that mimic bar demands. Retaking the LSAT to aim above average could also help if you reapply, as it directly sways these predictions. With your above-average GPA as a foundation, consistent effort in these areas could shift your profile toward the successful majority. Keep pushing—you have strengths to build on.
